name: Ceryneian Hind
description: The Ceryneian Hind was a mythical deer with golden antlers and bronze hooves, sacred to the goddess Artemis. It was incredibly swift, capable of outrunning any mortal or immortal. Heracles was tasked with capturing it alive as his third labor. This labor was particularly challenging, not only due to the Hind's speed, but also because Heracles was forbidden from harming it due to its sacred status. The pursuit lasted for an entire year, taking Heracles across Greece and beyond. Some accounts suggest Heracles wounded the Hind with an arrow before capturing it, while others claim he exhausted it. Ultimately, he brought the Hind back to Eurystheus, completing his labor. The Hind's capture demonstrated Heracles' strength, endurance, and respect (or lack thereof, depending on the account) for the divine. The golden antlers and bronze hooves further emphasized its unique and divine nature, setting it apart from ordinary deer. The pursuit itself became a testament to Heracles' legendary dedication to his tasks, even when faced with seemingly insurmountable challenges and divine restrictions. The Ceryneian Hind represents the wild, untamed aspects of nature and the delicate balance between the mortal and divine realms.
subtitle: Golden-antlered, bronze-hoofed sacred deer
appearance: The Ceryneian Hind was a magnificent deer, distinguished by its golden antlers and bronze hooves. Its coat was likely a natural deer color, providing camouflage in its woodland habitat. Its size was probably larger than that of a typical deer, befitting its mythical status. The golden antlers gleamed brightly, a symbol of its sacred connection to Artemis. The bronze hooves allowed it to traverse any terrain with ease, contributing to its unmatched speed and agility. Overall, its appearance was both beautiful and formidable, reflecting its divine nature and elusiveness.
habitat: Forests and mountains of Ceryneia, Greece.
behavior: The Ceryneian Hind was known for its incredible speed and elusiveness. It was a creature of the wild, roaming freely and avoiding contact with humans. As a sacred animal of Artemis, it likely exhibited a sense of pride and independence. Its primary behavior centered around survival and evading capture, making it a formidable challenge for any hunter.

keyMyths.title: Heracles' Third Labor
keyMyths.description: Heracles was tasked with capturing the Ceryneian Hind alive as his third labor. The pursuit lasted a year, testing his endurance and respect for the divine.

keyMyths.title: Sacred to Artemis
keyMyths.description: The Ceryneian Hind was a sacred animal to the goddess Artemis, making it forbidden to harm. This added a layer of complexity to Heracles' task.

sources.author: Apollodorus
